characters.name: open/close all folders
characters.description: General tropes about the main family of the series. All of the Other Reindeer: In one episode, they discover that their entire neighborhood has a block party whenever they go on vacation. Hal is the least hated of them, since his neighbors seem to dislike him only when he's around his wife. Antagonistic Offspring: The boys have implicitly all attempted tokill Lois, given they put Jamie on a pedestal for having been the one to come the closest. At Least I Admit It: In "Malcolm Babysits," Malcolm decides he'd rather be with his openly dysfunctional family than the seemingly wholesome, but backstabbing and secret-keeping, family he had enjoyed babysitting for. A running theme of the show is that they're not so much worse than other people as they either don't bother or are incapable of hiding their faults. Aw, Look! They Really Do Love Each Other: It's very much aSadist Show, but even at their most destructive, there are still frequent moments that remind the audience they are still a family.

characters.name: Malcolm
characters.description: The titular hero and occasional to-the-camera narrator, Malcolm, is the middle child in a large, dysfunctional, lower-middle-class family. He also has a genius-level IQ—but rather than making his life easier, it, in fact, makes it considerably more miserable, thanks to the neurosis he develops and the degrees of separation it instills between him, his family, his peers, and society in general. Indeed, most episodes of Malcolm in the Middle revolve around the self-made catastrophes our hero becomes embroiled in. His victories are almost always of thePyrrhicor bittersweet variety, and more than any other character, he's made to suffer. As the series progressed, Malcolm developed from being a relatively normal young boy into a considerably more cynical, neurotic individual.Justifiedfirstly by what he endures, and secondly because puberty ensued. Allegedly Dateless: Malcolm has plenty of dates and girlfriends in the show (though few of these last more than one episode), but he's still always referred to as a pathetic loser with no social life. And Starring: "And Frankie Muniz". Author Avatar: Malcolm is based on the series' creator, Linwood Boomer, as a kid.

characters.name: Lois
characters.description: The unquestioned head of the household, Lois is a very temperamental and overbearing woman. She is this because (to her mind) her borderline uncontrollable sons and scatterbrained husband would destroy themselves otherwise. It is demonstrated frequently that she loves her family more than anything and would (and does) do anything for them. It doesn’t save them from the fact that she makes their lives a living nightmare as an unwanted, unlucky necessity. Abusive Parents:Aside from the well-knownFinancial Abuse, she is also implied to be physically abusive, given what Francis commented on regarding what things his brothers should resist in regard to possible methods of extracting who burned her dress and his being completely unfazed by the tortures a cult puts him through, which resulted in their adopting Lois' methods. Of course, then again, given his obvious hatred towards his mother, it's possible that he eitherlied about itorled himself to believe it(though she herself says in the pilot that she gives the boys spankings, so it's reasonable to suspect she either did it while they were little, gave up on it because it clearly wasn't working, or it happens off-screen).She originally intended to subvert it, as she intended to make her household lax of rules specifically because she didn't want to have her children go through the life she herself had to put up with Ida. Unfortunately, Francis as a child proved how faulty that line of thought was when he did several bad things, which came to a halt when he attempted to douse his teddy bear with gasoline and set it on fire.Lois herself was abused by her own mother, who is shown to be far,farworse than Lois ever gets with her sons. And unlike Lois (who mainly acts this way in order to keep her admittedly rambunctious boys safe), Ida is just a horrible, spitefulJerkass. Aside from the well-knownFinancial Abuse, she is also implied to be physically abusive, given what Francis commented on regarding what things his brothers

characters.name: Hal
characters.description: Malcolm's father and Lois's obsessive, slightly unstable husband. Easily distracted and prone to extremely vocal breakdowns, episodes frequently center around his fly-by-night obsessions, which are mostly attempts to break out of the mundanity and squalor of his day-to-day life. He is devoted to Lois and delegates all of the running of the household to her. Action Dad: He has his moments when the occasion calls for them. Amazingly Embarrassing Parents: Like Lois, he has embarrassed his children more than once by being naked or half-naked. He has also put his children in more than embarrassing situations, such as in "Houseboat," where he slaps a woman's rear due to having mistaken her for Lois and calls Malcolm for help while being arrested, and Malcolm is trying to impress a girl, causing Malcolm to pretend to not know him. Angrish: Hal is reduced to furious incoherence on a few memorable occasions. Babies Ever After: Confirmed in the finale, much to his and his wife’s horror. In the revival this child is revealed to be Kelly.

characters.name: Francis
characters.description: The oldest of Hal and Lois's sons, Francis is a conniving delinquent who spends almost all of the series away from home - first at military school, then in Alaska, then at a Midwestern ranch. Episodes frequently split between Malcolm and whatever Francis is up to, making him a kind of star of a show-within-the-show. Similarly to Malcolm, Francis is quite smart and warm when he wants to be and even honorable up to a point, but is quite self-destructive and obsessed with one-upping his mother. Advertised Extra: In the last two seasons he's always credited as a main character, but only appears in 11 out of 44 episodes. Even before that, there are times where he only appears in a single scene in an episode, offering his own commentary over the phone to another member of the family on their plot fit the episode. Aesop Amnesia:Subverted. In Season 6, after being fired from the Grotto, he's back to the way he was during the first few seasons... Or so it seemed, turns he had a legitimate office job all along, which he admits to enjoying, he was actually only pretending to have turned back into an irresponsible troublemaker for the sake of screwing with Lois. Antagonistic Offspring: He's constantly at odds with Lois and considers her his biggest adversary. Anti-Role Model: His brothers idolize him, despite him being a messed up (former) delinquent. Reese, who looks up to Francis, could have gotten his delinquent tendencies from him.

characters.name: Reese
characters.description: The second oldest of Hal and Lois's sons. Easily the stupidest and most violent of the clan, he fills in as dumb muscle or a sucker for Malcolm and Dewey's schemes... or he bullies the hell out of them, as he sees fit. Although Reese always had an idiotic puppy kind of charm, as the series progressed he developed from being a moronic fall guy into a slightly more nuanced and likable character. Allegedly Dateless: Like Malcolm, he is a "loser" who is not well liked by anyone, but manages to have several short-term girlfriends during later seasons. Ambiguously Bi: Reese has many girlfriends and crushes, but also shows a certain... interest in boys. Anti-Hero: Starts off as aNominal Hero, being a vicious bully.Character Developmentsees him becoming more of anUnscrupulous Hero. Berserk Button: Even though he and Stevie arefrenemies at bestand sitcom arch-enemies at worst,seeing Stevie get bullied will not only genuinely piss Reese off,he'll also give the offending bully a taste of their own medicinesince Reese declared Stevie off-limits due to his disability.

characters.name: Dewey
characters.description: The youngest of Hal and Lois's sons, at least until Jamie comes along. Dewey is a quiet, devious, and rather spooky character who is frequently underestimated (or flat-out ignored) by the rest of his family. He became slightly less weird and more assertive as the series went on. Annoying Younger Sibling: To Malcolm and Reese, mostly in early seasons. They both hate spending time with him and see Dewey as a nuisance. Anti-Hero: Depending on the episode, he shifts between being aKnight in Sour Armoror aPragmatic Hero. Apple of Discord: Spends an entire episode asking seemingly innocuous questions to Hal and the other buddies in his acapella group (e.g. "How come you're always in the back?") that immediately breed anger and resentment to the point that they temporarily disband. He also does the same thing to a pair of dancers, who are later seen arguing in the background. Big Brother Instinct: Dewey will do everything in his power to protect and nurture the kids in the special classroom because no one else is willing to. Also to his younger brother, even tricking his own parents to ensure that the younger brother gets the attention from his parents that he himself never got because they were always too busy dealing with their more troublesome kids.

characters.name: Jamie
characters.description: Lois and Hal's youngest son, born halfway through the series. Although obviously extremely young, he seems destined to follow in his brothers' chaotic footsteps. Ambiguous Gender: He was born in the penultimate episode of Season 4 but his gender wasn't revealed until the opening of the fifth season. The Baby of the Bunch: In Season 4, Dewey's role as the youngest child is given to Jamie (who in turn passes it on to Kelly in the revival). Bicep-Polishing Gesture: Fond of doing this in the revival. Evidently, his time in the Coast Guard gave him impressive pecs. Cute Mute: He can bereallycute despite all the horrible things he does. And he hardly talks at all, aside from his first words(telling Lois to shut up).

characters.name: Kelly
characters.description: Hal and Lois’s nonbinary youngest child. Lois was revealed to be pregnant with them in the last episode of the original season series. The Baby of the Bunch: The youngest of Hal and Lois' six children. Daddy's Girl: They are very close to Hal because they always cheered them up as a kid since they started having issues regarding their gender identity at five years old and he was the one who comforted them on a bad day they had on fifth grade that made them realize they were non-binary. Their tribute to Hal is the most emotional, showing how much they adore their father. Messing with Hal is one way to get on their bad side. Deadpan Snarker: Kelly is very sarcastic and dismissive. Extremely Protective Child: TheirSibling Rivalrywith Reese starts when they discover Reese made embarrassing videos about Hal and profited from them. They intended to tell their parents about it, but Hal found out while having a breakdown over Malcolm abandoning the family.

characters.name: Leah
characters.description: Malcolm's daughter, born from a one-night stand during the gap between the original show and the revival. Fourth-Wall Observer: Just like her father, turning to the camera to comment on things. Friendless Background: Much like her father, she is anInsufferable Genius. Unlike her father she isn’t surrounded by other awkward nerds and does not have the social skills to mitigate her behavior. Like Parent, Like Child: She's inherited Malcolm'sDeadpan Snarkertendencies and social awkwardness around her peers, as well as his ability to trick others. Missing Mom: She was born from her father's one-night stand with a hippie woman named Dreamer, who abandoned the two of them three days after Leah's birth.

characters.name: Piama Tananahaakna
characters.description: Francis's wife whom he married off-screen. Berserk Button: Do NOT try to evict her from her home, or treat Francis badly. And while she handles it withTranquil Fury, she doesn't like it when Francis starts appropriating her native culture for personal profit. Birds of a Feather: Possibly why her and Francis fell in love so quickly, both had difficult childhoods including terrible relationships with their opposite sex parent figure, and a willingness to break the law, but are ultimately good people to those who treat them well. Characterisation Marches On: HerHair-Trigger Temperseems to fade over time, and her relationship with Francis goes fromLike Parent, Like Spouseto her being more soft and supportive. Also while in her first appearance she was shown not taking Lois' crap and standing up to her, she becomes more of a doormat in later appearances, taking Lois' barbs with silent acceptance until eventually hitting a breaking point when she makes a remark at the wrong time. Child Hater: A couple of episodes display that Piama is against the idea of having kids.Subvertedlater in the finale, as she claims to have gotten over it.

characters.name: Ida
characters.description: Lois's mother, the reason why she is the way she is. Abusive Parent: To Lois. In "Grandma Sues", she attempts to sue her. Even after Lois and Hal found out Lois was pregnant again, she simply blamed them and continued her lawsuit. She was only prevented from doing so when her lawyer refused to help her after he realized he would get nothing out of it. She also insults Lois frequently and openly, something which Lois recounts in "Stevie vs. Reese" had major effects on her self-esteem and body image as a teenager. And There Was Much Rejoicing: One of the first scenes in the revival confirms that she passed on in the interim between series. None of the family is shown to be sad about it. During herfuneral, Francis and Malcolm even get into a minor spat involving Francis having to watchDexterwith her back when she was alive, and swearing that she had a plasma TV while Malcolm points out it was an LED, all while carelessly dumping her ashes on the ground. Arch-Enemy: While most of the family hates her in the way one would a difficult relative, she and Francis are comic-book-level rivals; in the finale, Francis nearly kills her, and shegoads him into the fight. It's implied this happens often, and she gives as good as she gets physically, such as stabbing him in the leg. Baby Trap: Done hilariously when Mr. Li leaves her at the altar in "Grandma's Boyfriend" after the opioids she was drugging him with wear off, and Ida tries to claim to be pregnant to keep him from leaving, despite her extreme age.

characters.name: Victor
characters.description: Lois's father, who is just as bad as his wife. Abusive Parent: To Lois. She reveals to Reese that when teaching her how to ride a bike, he just tied her to one and shoved her down a hill. Evil Oldfolks: Just like his wife Victor is a very nasty, cruel and spiteful individual who treats everyone save his wife and Reese like crap. Gruesome Grandparent: Just like his wife, he's a cruel old dick to Francis and Malcolm.Technically they aren't his grandchildren. I Am Not Your Father:In the episode "Victor's Other Family", Ida confesses to Lois that she had cheated on Victor numerous times and that Lois' biological father was a man named Radu Gogorsky.

characters.name: Walter
characters.description: Hal's rich father who emotionally neglected his son. Abusive Parent: Not in the physical or verbal way, but the fact that he never accommodated to Hal's emotional needs, instead dodging any uncomfortable topics with humor and tickling makes him an emotional example. His death even caused Hal to be easily manipulated by his sons into doing them outrageous favors out of fear of history repeating itself with them and their bond. Bus Crash: In "Hal Grieves." He dies offscreen and his death becomes the kickstarter for the plot as Hal's grief makes him vulnerable to being manipulated by his sons in a misguided attempt to be closer to them unlike his father was with him. Bystander Syndrome: One of the major reasons Hal hardly visits. Walter himself is very kind to Lois, but he either doesn't notice or pretends not to notice the rest of the family being awful to her. Calling the Old Man Out: Hal eventually does this while wearing a thick layer of coats so that Walter could not simply stop him by tickling him.

characters.name: Hal's Family
characters.description: Hal's rich side of the family, headed by his emotionally neglectful father. They proceed to make Lois's life hell out of spite. They appear in "Family Reunion". Hate Sink: The vast majority of them are emotionally abusive, cruel and spiteful towards Lois going as far as keeping her out of the extended family photo by giving her a bunch of wild goose chases and mocking her when she cries over realizing how badly she's gotten screwed over. This makes Malcolm and the boys' subsequent destruction of their party all the more satisfying. Laser-Guided Karma: For all the grief they brought to Lois and driving her to tears, the boys ruin their party and get away unscathed from the chaos they made.To add salt to their wounds, “Hal Grieves” revealed that Walter died having lost all of his money, meaning that none of them ended up inheriting any of it when he passed. To add salt to their wounds, “Hal Grieves” revealed that Walter died having lost all of his money, meaning that none of them ended up inheriting any of it when he passed. Obnoxious In-Laws: Most of them are unpleasant and spiteful towards Lois for no reason beyond the fact that they can. Especially Aunts Amelia and Claire.

characters.name: Susan
characters.description: Lois's younger sister. Ambiguously Related: After Lois finds out that she isn't Victor's biological daughter because Ida cheated on hima lot, Lois asks about Susan. Ida only replies "anything is possible". The Glorious War of Sisterly Rivalry: Lois and Susan have a bitter relationship because Ida favors Susan and has always compared her favorably against Lois. Much later it's revealed that Lois also did things against Susan, like stealing her boyfriend… who happens to be Hal. They finally solve their differences when Lois finds out that Susan is dying from kidney failure, and she donates her kidney to save her life. Honor Before Reason: Susan pays a visit to Lois's family despite her bitter relationship with her sister. When Lois finds out about Susan's illness, Susan refuses to ask for or even accept her kidney because she would rather die than owe anything to Lois. It took Lois taking out her kidney anyway and forcing her to accept it to finally make up. Parental Favoritism: She's on the receiving end from both Ida and Victor, to the point Lois was often used as a slave to cater to Susan on her pageants.

characters.name: Victor's Second Family
characters.description: The secret family of Victor, Lois's father, whom he spent his entire life pampering and showering with gifts and kindness, a side of him which he never dared to show to Lois and Ida.It's likely this was because he wasn't even her real father. The Ace: Jerome, Roberta's younger son, is everything Malcolm is: handsome, intelligent and every bit as keen, but without the social and emotional insecurities that constantly hamper Malcolm. Bait-and-Switch: We're led to think that this is what Malcolm's family would have been like had they grown under the positive influence of a loving grandfather (and, subsequently, a loving mother). Roberta is even a dead ringer for Lois in appearance, just more well kept and with a rounder face.Then it turns out that no, they don't share the most minimal amount of DNA with them, and all their similarities are coincidence. Innocently Insensitive: Roberta’s comments when she looks at Lois childhood photos show how rigid her image of her father is. “I can’t find a single picture here where you're being serious.”noteThe family was dressed in rags and frightened-looking, which she mistakes for costumes. "Not So Different" Remark: Sylvia is on the receiving end of one from Lois. Ida is a pathetic, old woman who just wants to feel some connection with the man she spent years married to, and as such will go to the ends of the earth trying to claim a worthless pension ($43 Canadian dollars) just to prove shewasVictor's wife. However, Sylvia absolutely refuses to entertain the idea, completely ignoring Lois when she presses the issue and how it would mean Ida's financial suicide. This eventually leads to Lois realizing how, despite all her performative kindness, Sylvia is every bit as vindictive and spiteful as Ida is. Sylvia is shaken so hard by this, that she offers to sign anything Lois asks to, as long she doesn't compare them ever again.

characters.name: Dreamer
characters.description: The Ghost: She isn’t in the picture. The most we see of her is a quick shot of a photo. Parental Abandonment: She abandoned Leah pretty much as soon as she was born.

characters.name: Stevie Kenarban
characters.description: Malcolm's best friend and classmate. Beware the Nice Ones:When Reese abuses him with the Circle Game too many times, Stevie begins to finally get revenge.In another episode, Stevie uses a robotic exoskeleton (which he controls with his tongue) to beat up Reese. Now keep in mind, Reese temporarily paralyzed his legs because he thought Stevie would fight fair. He instantly knew he was in trouble when Stevie used the exoskeleton tobreak down the garage door. When Reese abuses him with the Circle Game too many times, Stevie begins to finally get revenge. In another episode, Stevie uses a robotic exoskeleton (which he controls with his tongue) to beat up Reese. Now keep in mind, Reese temporarily paralyzed his legs because he thought Stevie would fight fair. He instantly knew he was in trouble when Stevie used the exoskeleton tobreak down the garage door. Black and Nerdy: He's Malcolm's black friend from his class of geeks.

characters.name: Dabney Hooper and Lloyd Jensen
characters.description: Malcolm and Stevie's classmates. Abusive Parents: Lloyd comments that his mother calls him a failure in one episode. Dabney's mother insults him for not being as smart as Malcolm, only buys him birthday presents that he can use on her and nonsensically rants about how she knew letting him have friends was a bad idea at one point. Alcoholic Parent: Dabney mentions that his mom will drink to the point of passing out. Ambiguously Gay: Both are effeminate (Lloyd moreso) and are hinted at being attracted to men on a few occasions. It'sstillambiguous in the revival, where they and fellow Kreylboyne Kevin are raising a son together; but seem to dodge the question when asked if theyused a surrogateand it's unknown if they're romantically involved with each other or just very socially awkward. Break the Cutie: In "Emancipation". They start the episode trying to impress their new teacher, but as the episode progresses, they become so broken by Herkabe's ranking system that being outsmarted by Malcolm is enough to send them on a massive meltdown.

characters.name: Cynthia Sanders
characters.description: A classmate of Malcolm who is his first crush. Badass Israeli: She has been trained in IDF defensive combat at some point in the past and is able to beat upReesewith little effort. Beware the Nice Ones: She's a very sweet person but is highly trained in Krav Maga and not afraid to use it. Beware the Silly Ones: Has a very goofy and spacey personality and can effortlessly beat up Reese. Buxom Beauty Standard: In "Cynthia's Back", Cynthia comes back from Europe having undergone puberty and developing large breasts, which naturally changes how boys perceive her, including Malcolm who has an"All Grown Up" Remarkwhen noticing it and Reese who starts objectifying her and becomes obsessed with seeing or touching her boobs.She isn't a fan of all this attention.

characters.name: Eric Hanson
characters.description: Francis' best friend at Marlin, and often his voice of reason. Butt-Monkey: Somewhat, but not nearly as much as Francis until his final appearance where he's left with nothing but the clothes on his back and is trying to hitchhike while Francis and Piama leave Alaska behind in a sports car with the money they got selling Piama's childhood home. Know-Nothing Know-It-All: He got legally emancipated so he could leave school early and made his way out to Alaska, telling Francis about lucrative job opportunities that made the place seem like paradise. By the time Francis reaches the state and the logging camp Eric works in, Eric breaks down in tears revealing Alaska is far worse than military academy ever was due to what a horrible boss Lavernia is. Laser-Guided Karma: After everything Francis suffered through while working for Lavernia, he basically ditches Eric to rot as payback since Eric convinced him getting emancipated and moving to Alaska was a great idea. One-Steve Limit: Averted. He shares the same surname with one of Dewey’s friends, David Hanson.

characters.name: Stanley Winn
characters.description: A large intimidating cadet of Marlin Academy who becomes Francis' alleged bodyguard and one of his close friends during his first years at the academy. Chuck Cunningham Syndrome: Disappears after Season 2. The Dreaded: Nobody at Marlin Academy wants to get on his bad side. AGang of Bulliesat the school shamelessly backed off when he gave them little more than a stern look. Even Francis seems somewhat scared of him. Informed Attribute: His status as Francis's bodyguard. He's only protected Francis a couple of times and is in factmore likely to haze Francis himself for not going along with academy camping trips and the like. Mathematician's Answer: When he advices Francis to stop calling his girlfriend and Francis refuses to listen, Stanley has to resort to threatening him with the thumb, to which Francis asks if it’s the thing he does with his own thumb or what he does to other’s. He then approaches him with his thumb up, and then the scene cuts to Francis’s thumb heavily bandaged.

characters.name: Jessica
characters.description: Babysitter from Hell: Introduced as this in "Stereo Store". The Chessmaster: Jessica is good with complex plans that manipulate Malcom and Reese into doing things that will benefit her. Deadpan Snarker: She can outsnark even Malcolm. Karma Houdini: For the most part, she likes to mess with people and never gets punishments. Manipulative Bitch: Her defining trait, at least to Malcolm and his brothers.Lois, who is herself aManipulative Bitch, is often able to see right through her. However, she likes her anyways because she is one of the few people who can effectively force (or at least trick) the boys to get along with each other.

characters.name: Richie
characters.description: Francis' best friend before military school. Basement Dweller: He mooches off his mother, and lives in her barely habitable basement. It is implied that the place wouldn't be such a pigsty in the first place if not for Richie's general lack of hygiene. Delinquents: One of Francis' hoodlum friends before military school. Hypocritical Humor: He once berated Francis for being too dumb at times... Right before dumping a whole cannister of fuel into his garbage can bonfire. Put on a Bus: Disappears after Season 3, though he's mentioned once in Season 6.The Bus Came Back: He eventually returns in the second episode ofLife's Still Unfair, having turned his life around to become a practitioner of alternative medicine.

characters.name: The Buseys
characters.description: Kids from the "Disturbed Children" class who befriend Dewey. Character Tics: Hanson goes around in an invisible motorcycle. Chuck Cunningham Syndrome: They disappear without trace or explanation in Season 7. "Malcolm Defends Reese" features the appearance of a girl in Dewey's class who is clearly not a Busey, implying he simply moved to a different class. Evil Redhead: Chad, even thought he is not evil, just very crazy. He is always wearing a paper sign with warnings to not get closer to him, and his parents have to restrain him before bed time. Living Emotional Crutch: The entire class made Dewey this, since not even the teachers care for them. When Dewey takes a day off or is close to transferring to the normal class, the kids go berserk.

characters.name: Craig Feldspar
characters.description: Lois' co-worker who is deeply infatuated with her. Abhorrent Admirer: To Lois. When he confesses his love to her, she makes itveryclear she's not interested. His characterization eventually shifts from this to just being a sort of friend to the family and he even begins dating other women with varying degrees of success. Acrofatic: He plays softball, and is shown to beverygood at it. Until he gets a phone call mid-game, that is. All Love Is Unrequited: He's got a very obvious crush on Lois and resents Hal for marrying her. Lois is completely oblivious to it until he tells it to her face, at which that point she makes it clear that only the most unlikely of scenarios would she give him a sliver of a chance to consider it. Big Eater: Doesn't come up often surprisingly, given his largeGeek Physique, but when Hal needed Craig to help him buy a comic book, he had to take Craig around town buying him coffee, lunch, slushies, pie, and ice cream, and that still was not enough to get Craig to help.

characters.name: Abe and Kitty Kenarban
characters.description: Beware the Nice Ones: Kitty is initially extremely meek and mild-mannered. After spending a night at dinner with Malcolm's family, she begins to emulate Lois' personality and becomes more vocal and confrontational. After the third season, she snaps and abandons Abe and Stevie to become a porn star. Black and Nerdy: Abe. Like father, like son. When Hal isn't capable of grieving his father's death, Abe can only think to haveLeonard Nimoyor other members of theStar Trek: The Original Seriescast to talk to Hal. The Bus Came Back: Kitty returns in Season 6 after being absent for two years. Extreme Doormat: Kitty before meeting and being influenced by Lois. Fat Best Friend: Hal later admits to Abe that he considers them to be best friends. By the revival,he lost a ton of weightfrom microdosing.

characters.name: Caroline Miller
characters.description: Chuck Cunningham Syndrome: After the Krelboynes accidentally make her go into labor in the parking lot, she stops appearing in the show. Cool Teacher: Especially to Malcolm, she tries very hard to be one. She genuinely cares about him. Demoted to Extra: Main character in Season 1, special guest star in Season 2. Later replaced by Herkabe. Hot Teacher: She's very good-looking. Who's Your Daddy?: It's implied her pregnancy was the result of an affair with the school janitor.

characters.name: Lionel Herkabe
characters.description: The Alleged Car: He drives an extremely crappy car that looks like it could literally fall apart at any moment. All for Nothing: In his final appearance, he blackmails Malcolm to lower the grade in his class (from an A+ to borderline A/A- since simply failing him would be deemed suspicious) as a way to keep his long standing high academic record that he took from another, much older student. When Malcolm gets tired of Herkabe humiliating Reese, and blackmailing him, he reveals to the school district that Herkabe made up a class to avoid gym and get the academic record, and the award is given back to the original student since she completed all the graduation criteria, which included Physical Education. WhenHerkabe enrolls in P.E. class to fulfill the graduation criteria and win his award back, Reese, who's in the same class,picks on Herkabe like he would any other student since they're on an even playing field, and it's heavily suggested that Malcolm will most likely end up winning the award anyway since Herkabe no longer has any leverage over him and academically speaking, Malcolm has bounced back from worse. Big Bad Ensemble: He and Ida are the closest things to main antagonists in the show besides Lois and Reese. Contrasting Replacement Character: When Herkabe is hired as the new teacher for the Krelboynes, the kids are shocked when he turns out to be a cynical, rude jackass, in contrast to Caroline's nurturing personality. Dean Bitterman: He gets promoted to Dean of Discipline at the high school.

characters.name: Hal's Poker Buddies
characters.description: Abe's friends who welcome Hal into their circle. Actually Pretty Funny:When Lois is in her crusade against the mascot of a beer label, who is a black janitor named "Slappy", for being too racist, all of them actually find it very funny, especially when Abe himself stands next to the cardboard standee while holding a mop and a six-pack.Lois is hesitant to ask them to scare racist Ida away by acting like they're next-door neighbors, but they think playing up racist stereotypes sounds fun. When Lois is in her crusade against the mascot of a beer label, who is a black janitor named "Slappy", for being too racist, all of them actually find it very funny, especially when Abe himself stands next to the cardboard standee while holding a mop and a six-pack. Lois is hesitant to ask them to scare racist Ida away by acting like they're next-door neighbors, but they think playing up racist stereotypes sounds fun. Badass Boast: Steve delivers one to Ida when he's had enough of her bigotry.Steve: Ok, you know what? See, here's the thing, "Lady". We're better than you. Every one of us in every way. I'm smarter than you, more educated, and I contribute more to society. I have a family that loves me, I live in a big house, drive a nice car, and make more money in a year than you've probably made your whole lifetime.

characters.name: Pete
characters.description: An old guy working for Lavernia at the logger's camp, he becomes part of Francis' friend circle there, though is often the preferred victim to their hazing. Butt-Monkey: Because of his old age, he's an easy target for Francis and the others' bullying and hazing, and his misfortunes are generally played for laughs. The Dog Bites Back: When the group gets trapped by a bear inside a cabin and he gets voted to fetch the truck despite it putting his life at risk, he gets thrown out when the bear has already gone away and gets to ditch his treacherous co-workers and leave them to rot in the cabin. Hidden Depths: In the episode where he orders aMail-Order Bride, Francis questions what he'll do if the woman ends up not liking him. Pete responds that he'd be hurt, but he would be patient and polite to her in the hopes that she'll grow to like him, a level of thoughtfulness that surprises Francis. Manchild:Exaggerated. He's way older than even Lavernia, but acts like a recently emancipated teenager/young adult like Francis and Eric; fittingly when Lavernia has the entire staff fired, he says he'll have to go back to living with his parents.

characters.name: Tristan
characters.description: Malcolm’s girlfriend in the revival. Dark and Troubled Past: While kidnapping Malcolm and bringing him to his parent’s anniversary, she reveals that she almost went to jail for attempted murder of her mother’s abusive boyfriend. It was self defense. Extremely Protective Child: When she was younger, she shot her mother’s abusive boyfriend. Full-Frontal Assault: She says that she was naked when she shot her mother’s abusive boyfriend. Due to this, the incident was published online and on some newspapers, and Malcolm recognizes her as "Titty-Titty-Bang-Bang." Like Parent, Like Spouse: She's at first glance a sweet, kinda ditzy person who hates discussing controversial subjects; dating a cynic who's prone to angry rants - essentially making them a gender-swapped Hal and Lois. She even has the hidden dark side Hal keeps supressed.

characters.name: Commandant Edwin Spangler
characters.description: All Psychology Is Freudian: He comes to accept while speaking with Francis that his hatred towards his own mother resulted in several of his failings in life, including his missing eye. The Bus Came Back: He returns one last time in "Dewey's Dog." After Francis left the academy, apparently the place tanked and he was left with no purpose, so he travels all the way to Alaska to find Francis and figure out what to do next. The Character Died with Him: The revival shows that Spangler died in the interim between both series, withDaniel von Bargenhaving passed in 2015. Dark and Troubled Past: Later on in "Dewey's Dog," it's revealed that he's actually an extremely depressed, broken man and that tormenting the cadets at military school was the only joy and purpose he had in life. Francis runs with this and gets him a job at a retirement home in Alaska, giving him free rein to torment the elderly in the same way he used to torment the military school cadets (complete with aCall-Backto when he punished everyone for something one person did). Drill Sergeant Nasty: Spangler's a strict and hard-nosed commander who's harsh on his cadets.

characters.name: Lavernia
characters.description: Arc Villain: She's this for a fair portion of the Alaska arc throughout Season 3, being the main reason Francis is stuck on a crappy job and purposefully making his life miserable for the hell of it. Bad Boss: She not only works all of her employees like dogs, but also charges them high fees for rent and other basic amenities (such as bedding and hot water), and willdeduct money from their paycheckswhen they fail to keep up with it. She does this on purpose so they have to stay and work for her until they pay her off, which she makes sure they can't do until the logging is finished at which that point she immediately lays off everybody without any prior warning. Even Evil Has Loved Ones: When Piama holds her parakeet hostage in order to get her to treat Francis better, it works very well in making her treat Francis with decency. That is, until Francis tries to do the "right thing" and return the bird to Lavernia but due to his ignorance the parakeet dies of frostbite, bringing things back to square one. Hair-Trigger Temper: She's always in a foul mood and it takes pretty much nothing to set her off. Hate Sink: Spangler was a pretty bad guy towards Francis but he had his reasons for it and in the end grew to respect Francis for his defiant and rebellious attitude. Lavernia has not a single ounce of respect towards Francis, abuses all her workers and spends her entire screentime making everyone else miserable for either the slightest or no reason at all.

characters.name: Otto and Gretchen Mankusser
characters.description: Benevolent Boss: Otto is extremely accommodating to his workers and is the first truly good authority figure Francis ever had in his life. Francis once refers to him as "the best boss he's ever had." The Character Died with Him: The revival reveals that Otto died in the interim between both series (With Gretchen revealing the cause to be a Puma attack), Kenneth Mars himself passed back in 2011. Consulting Mister Puppet: When their estranged son was a child, Otto would discipline him with "Schlupi," a sock puppet. In Gretchen's words, "Schlupi could say all the things that Otto couldn't." Francis eventually gets Otto to reconnect with said son using Schlupi, and later uses it himself to call Lois and tell her that he realizes that she always had his best interests in mind. Contrasting Replacement Character: Unlike Spangler and Lavernia, Otto and Gretchen are legitimately nice bosses (even if Otto is too naive) and treat Francis decently. While the prior two bosses Francis had (and Lois) encouraged Francis' development by virtue of convincing him to avoid ever being under people like them, Otto and Gretchen giving Francis a fair job at The Grotto allows him to grow into a responsible, honest and mature adult who turns his life around from his prior disastrous state to a much more stable one. Europeans Are Kinky: Francis rewrites a scene from a porno he mistakenly allowed to be filmed at The Grotto to keep it clean for their sake, only to find out they were disappointed that the scene contained no sex.

characters.name: Dave Spath
characters.description: The first reigning bully of Malcolm's school who targeted the latter after he became part of the Krelboyne class. Appears in the pilot. The Bully: Before Reese was established as the "Alpha Bully", Dave Spath was introduced as the terror of the school who intended for Malcolm to be one of his many victims before his defeat. Even Bullies Have Standards: Handicapped kids are off-limits for his bullying as per usual for the bullies at Malcolm's school. When Stevie pretends to have been hurt by his punch when it graces him, his reputation goes down in flames and he gets implicitly expelled for it. Starter Villain: The first antagonist faced by Malcolm (discountingReese and Lois), unlike most other bullies or jerks that came after him he has some standards and is easily dealt thanks to Stevie'sWounded Gazelle Gambit.

characters.name: Brotherhood of the Apocalypse
characters.description: A group of senior year students at Marlin Academy who start a hazing cult with their doll head idol "Poquito Cabeza" serving as their way of choosing victims. The Dreaded: Besides Stanley (who is the most feared student at Marlin) and initially Francis (until he briefly feared them when he invoked their wrath), all of the students fear them and lose all hope of escaping them when marked by "Poquito Cabeza", most notably Finley who gets to be the first onscreen victim. Ineffectual Sympathetic Villain: Stanley doesn't take them seriously unlike the rest of the school and when they try and intimidate Francis they fail miserably, primarily because the hellLoisput Francis through in his childhood is worse than anything they could come up with. This leads to them using her methods instead after hearing the whole spiel from Francis. Took a Level in Badass: By the end of their episode, they've become twice as effective thanks to following Lois' example.

characters.name: The New Neighbors
characters.description: A family of four that moves in during their titular episode in Season 2. Most of them are a terror and become instant enemies with the Wilkersons, with the exception of the father Mike who instead hits it off with Hal. Big, Screwed-Up Family: Somehow worse than Malcolm's family, the children are even crueler than Malcolm, Reese and Dewey and Tina is a cheating liar and once this is revealed, she and her children separate from a depressed Mike, moving away from both him and the neighborhood. Bratty Half-Pint: Emily frequently bites Reese, likely not knowing any better as Tina refuses to correct the behavior. Malicious Slander: Josh constantly spreads lies about Malcolm, even going as far as to claim he's a peeping tom. Obnoxious Entitled Housewife: Tina won't rear her children's callous behavior, and even treats a worker she hired to help her with her yard like a customer yelling at an employee for petty reasons.

characters.name: Mr. Woodward
characters.description: Reese's teacher in the Season 2 episode "Tutoring Reese". He turns out to be completely biased against Reese due to his bullying ways, deliberately failing him on every test and assignment regardless of the true accuracy and quality of his work and unjustly trying to get him put in the school's remedial class simply because of this. Bully Hunter: He is biased against Reese due to his status as a bully and it's strongly implied through his conversation with Malcolm about him flunking Reese on his history test (which Malcolm secretly took for him) that Reese isn't the only bully whose academic standing he has sabotaged throughout his career. Dark and Troubled Past: Doubles withFreudian Excuse. When Malcolm confronts Mr. Woodward over him giving a failing grade to Reese's passing test work (which unbeknownst to Woodward at the time, was the result of Malcolm cheating for him out of desperation), he reveals that his bias towards Reese stems from having been picked on bullies like him pretty severely during his adolescent years. He brings up one incident in particular where one of his bullies deliberately aimed for his legs in dodgeball; as a result, Woodward fell, broke his jaw, and had to have his mouth wired shut for weeks. This consequently caused his teeth to not set properly and forced him to have to wearextremely embarrassing dental headgearfor six years, which no doubt led to even more ridicule and bullying from both his bullies and other peers all throughout the rest his childhood, which he evidently never got any therapy or counseling for. Freudian Excuse Is No Excuse: Nevertheless, regardless of his tragic past, Woodward is still in the wrong for sabotaging the grades and efforts of other students like Reese, especially if they were making honest hard-working headway and effort like he was. If anything, one could even argue that his biased treatment makes said students even worse:if a bully or delinquent is only seen and treated like a failure and screwup even

characters.name: Oliver
characters.description: A butler monkey bought by Craig to help him while he's suffering the aftermath of his crippling injuries indirectly caused by Dewey's dog. He turns out to be much more malevolent than anyone could've expected. Bitch in Sheep's Clothing: A seemingly nice, helpful monkey who is actually a murderously evil primate out to torture and then kill Craig for the hell of it. For the Evulz: Torments and tries to kill Craig for no other reason besides the fact that he can and enjoys his attempted murders. Killer Rabbit: Smaller than either Craig or Hal but he puts them on the ropes through his sheer aggression, cruelty and clever use of the environment to pelt them with everything within his reach. Maniac Monkeys: A demented assassin capuchin monkey who tries to kill Craig and then Hal.

characters.name: "La Vaca Diablo"
characters.description: It was said that a long time ago a cow ate human flesh and became a terrifying cryptid that comes out when the Moon is blood-red. Its attacks on the Grotto prompt Francis and Otto to try and take it down. Herbivores Are Friendly:Zig-Zagged. It starts out destroying parts of the Grotto and killing any animal that it crosses paths with; but once Francis relieves her of her strained udder pain, she becomes a friendly cow. Real After All:Subverted. Otto is skeptical of its existence but when it almost attacks him, he takes it back; then when he and Francis try and confront it, the latter discovers that it's nothing more than a normal lost milk cow who needed to be relieved of her udder pain.

characters.name: Charles Cutler
characters.description: ACaustic Criticwhose bad reviews threaten to put businesses out of work, when his critical trashing of the Grotto threatens the place he's invited over by Francis to see it's not all bad. Appears in "Malcolm Films Reese". Hated by All: No one likes Charles Cutler. When Francis and Otto beat him down and he writes a scathing review about the Grotto, everyone in the state lauds them as heroes for finally giving him what he deserved. Hate Sink: In his short screen time, he becomes such a reviled, despicable jerk that you'll be cheering when Francis andOttoare done with him. Jerkass: One of the few people in the entire show who could give Ida a run for her money in being a despicable jackass, not only is he harsh beyond professionalism with his critiques, but he also will not doubt about insulting employees and being racist to them on top of it. Laser-Guided Karma: His insults initially just upset Francis and Otto, but once he decides to target Piama with his bigotry he gets beaten senseless by the former two; and the review he writes where he brings up their assault on him only help them gain more popularity, because he was such a hated figure that anyone who put him in his place becomes a hero.

characters.name: Mannfred Schmidt
characters.description: Otto's former best friend and Gretchen's former fiancee. When Otto unconsciously hurts himself in various accidents fueled by his guilt over stealing Gretchen from Mannfried after receiving a letter from him, Francis invites the latter so they can work things out. Appears in "Hot Tub". Uncertain Doom: Last we see of him is getting knocked over by a lamp into a metal cart and getting hit by lightning, while characters have survived a bunch of lethal-in-real lifeAmusing Injuries, it's unknown if Mannfred made it, since he doesn't appears again.

characters.name: Reese's Bullies
characters.description: AGirl Possethat plays a mean prank on Reese by catfishing him with Cindy and taking an embarrassing photo when it's revealed that "Cindy" is just a pig. Alpha Bitch: All four of them act like your typical high school snobby pretty girls. Asshole Victim: None of the four girls even feel sympathy for each other after Lois gets revenge on them. Diane even mocks Kristin after having to get her hair cut which makes their karma much more deserved. Hate Sink: They have no redeeming qualities and only exist for Lois to exact humiliating revenge on. Jerkass: All four are mean girls that will put themselves over everyone else.

characters.name: Lois' Imaginary Daughters
characters.description: Female counterparts to Lois' sons that she imagined to cope with the stress of her pregnancy with Jamie (whose sex was not yet revealed at this point) on top of the boys' misbehavior. Big Sister Instinct: Renee claims that Mallory's crush Jason, an older boy working at a pretzel shop in the mall, is no good for her and she should be with someone else. Subverted when it's revealed that Renee slept with Jason. Disproportionate Retribution: It's revealed that Renee slept with (and got pregnant by) Mallory's crush Jason as retaliation for Mallory using her hairbrush without permission. More Deadly Than the Male: When Lois' fantasy starts to come apart as the girls start turning on her and on each other, she tries to assert that girls are supposed to be better behaved and easier to manage, but they retaliate by saying that as girls, they know how she thinks and aren't above using it against her. Really Gets Around: Mallory claims that Renee has sex with different boys every night by the creek when Lois believed her to be at band practice, and when Daisy reveals that Renee is pregnant, she tries to deny it and says that sex is "wrong and immoral andfun-"
